hints.voluntas: 'Voluntas' is a noun meaning 'will' — it is the subject

steps.target: to become / to be made / to be done
choices: to become / to be made / to be done

hints.to make / to do: 'To make' is 'facere' — 'fiat' is from 'fieri' (the passive of 'facere')
hints.to trust: 'To trust' is 'fidere' — 'fiat' is from 'fio, fieri' (to become/be done)

steps.reveal: fiat = let [it] be done / may [it] happen (jussive subjunctive)

hints.present indicative: Present indicative would be 'fit' — 'fiat' with -a- is subjunctive
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'fiet' — 'fiat' is present subjunctive
hints.imperfect subjunctive: Imperfect subjunctive would be 'fieret' — 'fiat' is present

hints.facio, facere, feci, factum: These are the parts of 'facio' (to make) — 'fio' serves as its passive
hints.fido, fidere, fisus sum, —: These are the parts of 'fido' (to trust) — a semi-deponent
hints.fero, ferre, tuli, latum: These are the parts of 'fero' (to carry/bear)

steps.target: jussive subjunctive expressing a wish or command
choices: jussive subjunctive expressing a wish or command
steps.reveal: The subjunctive expresses Jesus' prayer: 'let your will be done'

hints.purpose clause after 'ut': There is no 'ut' — this is an independent jussive subjunctive
hints.indirect question: There is no interrogative word — this is a direct wish/command
hints.result clause: Result clauses use 'ut' + subjunctive — this is independent, expressing a wish

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'voluntatis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'voluntatis' ends in -is
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'voluntatis' ends in -is

steps.prompt: What is the understood subject of 'fiat'?
steps.reveal: Non mea [voluntas] sed tua [voluntas] fiat = Not my will but yours be done

hints.'ego' — I: The verb 'fiat' is 3rd person singular — 'I' would require 'fiam'
hints.'corpus' — body: 'Corpus' is not mentioned in this passage — the subject is 'voluntas'
hints.'Deus' — God: 'Deus' is not the grammatical subject — 'tua voluntas' (your will) is

steps.prompt: What grammatical feature is 'Non mea... sed tua'?
steps.target: ellipsis — 'voluntas' is understood with both 'mea' and 'tua'
choices: ellipsis — 'voluntas' is understood with both 'mea' and 'tua'

hints.ablative absolute: Ablative absolute requires ablative noun + participle — these are nominatives
hints.indirect statement: Indirect statement uses accusative + infinitive — this is a direct wish
hints.asyndeton: Asyndeton omits conjunctions — 'sed' is present as a conjunction here

hints.ablative feminine: Ablative feminine 'mea' looks the same, but it agrees with 'voluntas' (nom.)
hints.vocative feminine: Vocative would be for direct address — 'mea' modifies the subject 'voluntas'
hints.accusative neuter plural: Accusative neuter plural 'mea' is possible, but here it modifies 'voluntas' (fem. nom.)

translation: Not my will but yours be done
